title: Break a Task Into Steps
summary: Split a large task into smaller, actionable steps that are easier to start.
learnerPromise: You will be able to take an overwhelming task and turn it into a short list of small, doable actions.
whyItMatters: Large tasks can feel paralyzing. Breaking them down makes it easier to begin, creates a clear path forward, and gives you a sense of progress as you check items off.
realLifeUses: Turn 'clean my room' into specific steps like pick up clothes, make the bed, and put away books
Break a big school project into research, outline, draft, and revise
Split 'plan a party' into guest list, food, decorations, and invites
Divide job applications into update resume, find openings, write cover letter, and submit
materialsNeeded: Paper and pen, or a notes app
result: A written list of 3 to 7 small, specific steps for one task.
youWillLearn: How to identify the first small action in a large task
How to write steps that are clear and start with a verb
How to order steps in a logical sequence
How to make each step small enough to finish in one sitting
miniChallenge: Pick one task you have been avoiding and break it into at least 3 small steps on paper or in a notes app.
steps: Pick one task that feels too big or overwhelming to start.
Ask yourself: what is the very first small thing I would need to do?
Write that first step down, starting with an action verb like 'find,' 'gather,' or 'write.'
Continue listing the next small steps in order until the task is complete.
Look at your list and check that each step is small enough to do in one sitting.
completionCriteria: You have a written list of at least 3 steps
Each step starts with a verb and describes one small action
The steps follow a logical order from start to finish
commonProblems: Making steps too vague like 'do the thing' instead of a specific action
Making steps too big so each one still feels overwhelming
Listing too many steps and losing focus on what matters most
tips: If a step still feels hard, break it down even smaller
Start each step with a verb to make it feel actionable
Focus on the first two or three steps to get started, you can plan the rest later
